coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots internalization enhances electroactive biofilm formation and microbial acetate synthesis
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jiaping
Zeng, Cuiping
Liu, Guangli
Ren, Zhiyong Jason
Luo, Haiping
Teng, Min
coredata.dc:description: This study investigated how carbon dots (CDs) and N-doped CDs modification of acetogenic bacteria enhanced the extracellular electron transfer (EET) and the formation of electroactive biofilm, which subsequently improved acetate production from CO2 in microbial electrosynthesis (MES). Characteristic fluorescence emission peaks of CDs and N-doped CDs were detected inside and outside of bacterial cells, respectively, suggesting that the CDs were successfully internalized by the cells. The (N-doped) CDs modified acetogens increased current output by 89–95%, and acetate production rate by 57–71%. The highest acetate production rate and electron recovery efficiency reached 2.1 mM d−1 and 91.3%, respectively. Acetoanaerobium and Sporomusa, which are considered to have different EET strategies, dominated the biocathodes modified with CDs and N-doped CDs, respectively. The CDs modification increased biofilm thickness by 86% and reached 41.0 ± 1.3 μm, which could be related to the increased secretion of flavins that enhanced the signal transduction interspecies. With the N-doped CDs modified, the biofilm was occupied with active pyrrolic N/graphitic N (52%), and the expression of functional genes encoding cytochrome (cydB, cybH, CcdA) were enhanced significantly. This study offers insights into the EET mechanisms by acetogens and a viable approach to increase acetate production from CO2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acetogens
Carbon dots
Microbial electrosynthesis
Extracellular electron transfer
Biofilm formation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and N-doped CDs
Internalized CDs or N-doped CDs by acetogens
(N-doped) CDs modified acetogens enhanced MES performance
Biofilm formation and EET mechanisms in the MES
Microbial communities in the cathodic biofilms
Extracellular electron transfer strategies of the biofilms

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption spectra (black), photoluminescence excitation spectrum (PLE, blue), and photoluminescence emission spectrum (PL, red) of CDs and N-doped CDs (a–b); Size distribution of CDs and N-doped CDs (c–d); FTIR spectra and XPS survey of CDs and N-doped CDs (e–f); The growth curve and acetate accumulation of mixed culture acetogens with CDs and N-doped CDs modified (CK: only mixed culture acetogens) (g–h); Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and N-doped CDs distributed inside and outside bacterial cell (i–j) (CDs: Ex/Em = 395/460 nm; N-doped CDs: Ex/Em = 345/445 nm; Inset: the photographs of CDs and N-doped CDs solution under daylight lamp (left) and UV lamp irradiation (right) at Ex = 365 nm).

Current output in MESs (a); Acetate accumulation (b); Electron recovery efficiency for acetate production (c); (d) and (e) show Nyquist plots and internal resistances analyzed by fitting to the equivalent circuit for the cathodic biofilm via EIS (*, **, ***, and ns represent p < 0.05, p < 0.01, p < 0.001, and not significant, respectively).

Spatial distribution images of cathodic biofilm matrix structure in CK (a), CDs (b) and N-doped CDs (c) analyzed by CLSM; Total biomass and intracellular lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) for cathodic biofilm (d); Coverage rate (cell area/total image area, %) and microbial viability (ratio of live and dead cells to total cells) in inner (near the electrode, 0–13 μm), middle (average division position, 13–29 μm), and outer layers (close to the catholyte interface, 29–41 μm) of the cathodic biofilm (d–e) (Cell area calculation based on mean fluorescence intensity obtaining from five random CLSM data of each sample).
XPS full survey, and N 1s XP spectra of acetogenic biocathode in CK and N-doped CDs (a–c).

Microbial community composition of cathodic biofilm at genera and species levels, respectively (a–b); Hierarchical cluster analysis of the community structure difference among CK, CDs, and N-doped CDs (the relative abundance at genera and species was in the top 10).

3D-EEM fluorescence spectra of exctracted EPS from cathodic biofilm with CK, CDs, and N-doped CDs, respectively (a–c), and extracted fluorescence emission spectrum from Fig. 6a–c (d–f) (the normalized fluorescence intensity of which were obtained at EX = 290, 345, 360, 395, and 420 nm).
Electron transfer-related functions predicted according to high-throughput sequencing function prediction and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database by PICRUSt2.

row.entry: cytochrome d ubiquinol oxidase subunit II

head.ce:title: Carbon dots internalization enhances electroactive biofilm formation and microbial acetate synthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Remediation Technology, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Remediation Technology
School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Remediation Technology, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Quantitative Engineering Biology, Shenzhen Institute of Synthetic Biology, Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shenzhen, 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Quantitative Engineering Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Quantitative Engineering Biology, Shenzhen Institute of Synthetic Biology, Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Princeton University, Princeton, NJ, 08544, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Princeton University, Princeton, NJ 08544, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, 510006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigated how carbon dots (CDs) and N-doped CDs modification of acetogenic bacteria enhanced the extracellular electron transfer (EET) and the formation of electroactive biofilm, which subsequently improved acetate production from CO2 in microbial electrosynthesis (MES). Characteristic fluorescence emission peaks of CDs and N-doped CDs were detected inside and outside of bacterial cells, respectively, suggesting that the CDs were successfully internalized by the cells. The (N-doped) CDs modified acetogens increased current output by 89–95%, and acetate production rate by 57–71%. The highest acetate production rate and electron recovery efficiency reached 2.1 mM d−1 and 91.3%, respectively. Acetoanaerobium and Sporomusa, which are considered to have different EET strategies, dominated the biocathodes modified with CDs and N-doped CDs, respectively. The CDs modification increased biofilm thickness by 86% and reached 41.0 ± 1.3 μm, which could be related to the increased secretion of flavins that enhanced the signal transduction interspecies. With the N-doped CDs modified, the biofilm was occupied with active pyrrolic N/graphitic N (52%), and the expression of functional genes encoding cytochrome (cydB, cybH, CcdA) were enhanced significantly. This study offers insights into the EET mechanisms by acetogens and a viable approach to increase acetate production from CO2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetogens are widely distributed in natural habitats and play an integral role in the global carbon cycle. Due to their unique capability of CO2 capture and conversion to acetate with highly efficient Wood-Ljungdahl pathway, these microbes have been increasingly studied to enable a circular carbon economy (Rosenbaum and Müller, 2021). Some acetogens, such as Sporomusa strains and Clostridium strains, carry extracellular respiration capability, which allow them to obtain electrons directly or indirectly from the solid-state electron donors (e.g., Fe minerals or carbon electrode) to reduce CO2 to acetate (Philips, 2019). Such respiration process has been adopted in microbial electrosynthesis (MES) process to utilize renewable electrons for molecule synthesis. Acetate is the most common product from MES by acetogen, and it is a widely used commodity chemical and also an important precursor for chemicals with much higher values, such as functionalized aliphatic and aromatic compounds, alkanes, and complex natural products (Keasling, 2010; Jiang et al., 2018).

MES has high selectivity toward acetate compared to abiotic electrochemical conversion of CO2, but its development is still limited by low electron transfer efficiency (45–60%) (Xiang et al., 2017; Jack et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2022). A general approach is to modify the MES cathode to improve the electron transfer rate between the cells and the electrode. Conductive materials have been tested for this purposed as they have high specific surface areas and electrical conductivity. For example, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Jourdin et al., 2014), Mo2C (Huang et al., 2020), MoS2 (Song et al., 2020), and prussian blue nanotubes (Tian et al., 2020) have been used to modify the cathode to enhance acetate production rates, and the results showed a 87–233% increase compared with the control cathode without modification. However, the electron recovery efficiency in these studies ranged in 50–65%, which could be limited by the low coverage of microbial cells over the cathode and the effective distance of electron transfer between the cells and electrodes (Yu et al., 2020).

Nanomaterials may fix this cap by serving as conductive assistant (Yu et al., 2020), cooperating with cytochromes (Liu et al., 2015), activating extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) (Ye et al., 2018), and facilitating the formation of electroactive biofilms (Ren et al., 2019). Previous studies showed nanomaterials scattered inside and outside of microbial cells participated in the process of transmembrane EET and increased electron transfer rate (Yang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). For example, carbon dots (CDs) are biocompatible conductive nanomaterials that could be up-taken by Shewanella, which triggered the cellular physiological response to secrete abundant electron shuttles to boost outward EET (Liu et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020). Nitrogen (N) doped nanomaterials can enhance flavin-mediated and/or cytochrome-mediated outward electron transfer in Shewanella (Yu et al., 2015; You et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020). To date the EET studies of electroactive biofilm mainly focuses on the outward EET with the direction of EET from inside the cells to the electrodes, but for electrophic bacteria like acetogens, the direction of EET is from outside solid electron donor into the cells, namely inward EET (Lovley and Holmes, 2021). Inward EETs can be carried out by either mediated process via hydrogen (H2) (Philips, 2019) or artificial mediators (e.g., methyl viologen) (Song et al., 2011), or direct process via outer-membrane-bound redox proteins (e.g., cytochromes c) (Deng et al., 2018). The traditional electron transfer pathways of acetogens depend on the transmembrane ion gradient type (H+ and Na+) (Rosenbaum and Müller, 2021), but the role of cytochromes c and outer membrane proteins on the inward EET of acetogens are largely uncharacterized.

Therefore, in this study, we hypothesized that the inward EET can be boost by internalized CDs and N-doped CDs for acetogens to form a more robust electroactive biofilm and increase carbon conversion efficiency. We investigated the role of CDs and N-doped CDs during biofilm formation, and characterized the electrochemically active substances in the EPS. To connect electrochemical responses with microbial activities, we also performed microbial community analysis, advanced microscopy, and a suite of electrochemical characterizations, and we found CDs and N-doped CDs distributed inside and outside of cells indeed enhanced inward electron transfer and boosted electron to carbon conversion through different strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CDs and N-doped CDs with different mass ratio of citric acid and urea (3:0 and 3:1) by hydrothermal method was previously described by Wang et al. (2017). The brief description was as follows: 3.0 g citric acid was combined with 0 g urea and 1 g urea, respectively, and dissolved in 15 mL DI water. The above dissolved mixtures were labeled as CDs and N-doped CDs, respectively, then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined reactor, and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling at room temperature, the obtained CDs and N-doped CDs solutions were centrifuged at 10,000 g for 30 min to remove large particles. The obtained supernatant was adjusted to neutral pH with HCl. Subsequently, it was dialyzed by a pretreated dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut off 3.5 KD at 4 °C for 72 h, during which the dialysate was continuously stirred and replaced with DI water 6 times. Finally, 133 g L−1 CDs and 133 g L−1 N-doped CDs were obtained.

The sediment from the Pearl River (Guangzhou, China) was inoculated into sterile modified DSM 879 medium (adjusted 2 g L−1 NaHCO3, 0.1 g L−1 yeast extract; omitted D-fructose, resazurin sodium, cysteine and Na2S × 9H2O). Sub-cultured acetogens for more than 6 months were co-cultured with CDs (200 mg L−1) and N-doped CDs (200 mg L−1), respectively. Taking 4 d as a cycle, the H2–CO2 mixture (80/20, v/v) was introduced into the anaerobic bottle for 10 min every 24 h. All anaerobic bottles were placed on a constant temperature shaker (30 °C, 180 r min−1) for batch culture.

The glass bottles of the “H"-type MES reactor were separated into anodic chamber and cathodic chamber by a Nafion 117 proton exchange membrane (DuPont, USA). The pretreated graphite plates (length × width × thickness × quantity = 5 cm × 2 cm × 0.5 cm × 5) were insert into the cathodic chamber and the anodic chamber, respectively. The effective volume of each chamber was about 150 mL. Modified DSM 879 medium containing 200 mg L−1 CDs and 200 mg L−1 N-doped CDs was added to the cathodic chamber, respectively. The anolyte was consistent with previous studies (Xiang et al., 2017). A Hg/HgCl2 reference electrode (SCE, +0.245 V vs. SHE) was placed in the cathodic chamber, followed by bubbling N2–CO2 mixture (80/20, v/v) for 45 min to remove dissolved oxygen in the catholyte. The three-electrode system, with the cathode as the working electrode, the anode as the counter electrode, the Hg/HgCl2 as the reference electrode, was constructed using a multi-channel potentiostat (CHI1000C, CH Instruments, Inc., China). After inoculating 5 mL precultured acetogens into the cathodic chamber, the cathodic potential was set at −700 mV (vs. SHE). Within 0–6 d of the MES operation, the H2–CO2 mixture (80/20, v/v) was bubbled into the cathodic chamber for 10 min to accelerate acetogen enrichment on the cathodic surface, every 2 d. After replacing the fresh catholyte and anolyte for 6 d, the H2–CO2 mixture (80/20, v/v) was switched to the N2–CO2 mixture (80/20, v/v). The MES operated with a cycle of 4 d, during which pure CO2 gas was bubbled into the catholyte for 2 min every day to alleviate the pH change. The MES ran for about 34 d, and the addition of CDs and N-doped CDs was stopped. Subsequently, current output and acetate accumulation were measured during continuous operation for 4 cycles (34–50 d). After each aeration, 2.5 mL of acetylene was reinjected into the top of cathodic chamber or anaerobic bottle to inhibit methane production.

To measure the electrical properties of the materials, the CDs and N-doped CDs were loaded on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE), respectively. The peak potential difference of the activated GCE in CV test with potassium ferricyanide as electrolyte was < 80 mV, indicating that the GCE can be used. The material was coated on the GCE with the load of 0.5 mg cm−2. Measurements of CV, LSV, and EIS were performed for the CDs- and N-doped CDs-coated GCE using an electrochemical workstation (Donghua, Shanghai). Additionally, EIS measurements for the cathodic biofilms were performed at the end of MES operation. The parameters of EIS measurement was from 1 × 105 to 0.05 Hz at open-circuit potential with a sinusoidal perturbation of 5 mV amplitude. Data from EIS were obtained as Nyquist plots and fitted to an equivalent electrical circuit using ZsimDemo analysis software (Fig. S1). All electrochemical measurements were performed under anaerobic conditions, and repeated at least 3 times.

The cathodic biofilm was scraped from 3 pieces of graphite plates using a sterilized blade, transferred to a centrifuge tube containing 10 mL of NaCl solution and 10–15 sterile glass beads; the above suspension was vortexed at high speed for 2–3 min, and then centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min at 4 °C; the repeated washed cells at the bottom of the centrifuge tube were resuspended in 500 μL 0.9% NaCl solution, placed in a water bath at 55 °C for 30 min, centrifuged at 8000 g for 15 min at 4 °C; the collected supernatant was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter and stored as “EPS”; the extracted EPS from cathodic biofilm was stored at 4 °C for further spectroscopic analysis. Furthermore, residual cells after water bath and centrifugation were resuspended in a centrifuge tube containing 300 μL of 0.2 N NaOH and 6–8 sterile glass beads, vortexed at high speed for 5 min, centrifuged at 12,000 g for 6 min at 4 °C; the collected supernatant was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter and used to characterize fluorescence emission spectrum inside the bacterial cells.

The functional group information about the synthesized material was obtained on a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (USA) with a FTIR spectral range of 4000–400 cm−1, 64 scans, and a resolution of 8 cm−1. Particle size distributions of CDs and N-doped CDs were determined using a particle sizer analyzer (Omni, Brookhaven, USA). The chemical composition on the surface of freeze-dried material (CDs, N-doped CDs) and biocathode (5 mm × 5 mm) was characterized by using XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, UK). For XPS data, the binding energy of the atoms were calibrated against a C1s of 284.8 eV and deconvolution of N 1s peaks was further performed by Gauss fitting in software (Avantage 4.15). The morphology of the (N-doped) CDs was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, Japan) working on 200 kV. Drops of a dilute aqueous solution of the samples were deposited on a carbon-coated copper grid for TEM. The crystal structure of the (N-doped) CDs was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku SmartLab, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm). Raman spectra were recorded using an inVia Qontor confocal Raman microscope (Renishaw Plc., Gloucestershire, UK) with laser excitation source at 532 nm. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600i, Shimadzu, Japan).

The fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized material and the extracted EPS was obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4, HORIBA, USA). The dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3D-EEM) fluorescence spectra of the extracted EPS were obtained, and the normalized fluorescence intensity (FI) was obtained by correcting FI to the Raman peak at Ex = 350 nm. Protein of cathodic biofilm was determined using Bradford protein kit (Beyotime, China). The cell permeability of cathodic biofilm was assessed using the LDH Cytotoxicity Assay Kit (Beyotime, China).

Five graphite plates (each 5 mm × 5 mm) were randomly cut from the biocathode, rinsed with sterile 0.9% NaCl solution, and then dyed in PI/SYTO fluorescent (Baclight L7012, Invitrogen, USA) for 20 min in the dark. Subsequently, the thickness and vitality of cathodic biofilm was observed using a laser confocal microscope (LSM 800, ZEISS, Germany). Image-Pro Plus 6.0 software was used to calculate the pixels of live and dead cells to obtain microbial coverage and vitality. At the end of MES operation, the cathodic biofilm was scraped from 2 pieces of graphite plates using a sterilized blade, and used to analyze the cathodic microbial community structure. All procedures including total DNA extraction, amplification, library construction, sequencing, and data analysis for biofilm samples were performed by Biomarker Technologies Corporation in Beijing, China. The full-length bacterial 16S rRNA gene sequence was amplified using the barcoded conserved primers 27F (5′-AGAGTTTGATCCTGGCTCAG-3′) and 1492R (5′-GGTTACCTTGTTACGACTT-3′). The amplified library was sequenced with PacBio Sequel II SMRT sequencing technology. Subsequently, the microbial sequencing data was visualized at the BMK Cloud platform (www.biocloud.net). Moreover, CO2 fixation- and electron transfer-related functional genes of the cathodic microbial community were predicted by PICRUSt2 based on the 16S rRNA gene marker and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database. With a larger database of reference genomes and gene families, PICRUSt2 as a widely used algorithm has optimized genome prediction and improved accuracy of functional predictive analysis (Douglas et al., 2020).

Acetate concentration was determined with ion chromatography (CIC-D100, Shenghan, China), equipped with anion IC column (SH-AC-4, Shenghan, China). The electron recovery efficiency for acetate production was calculated by bnF/Q, where b is the moles of electron (8 electrons equivalent per mole of acetate), n is the mole number of acetate production, F is Faraday's constant (96,485C·mol−1), and Q is the integral of current over time (C). The data were presented as the mean ± standard error (n ≥ 3). SPSS 26.0 (IBM, USA) was used to statistically analyze whether the MES performance among groups were statistically significant, using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by multiple comparisons (LSD) at p < 0.05 (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internalized CDs or N-doped CDs by acetogens
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, with UV lamp irradiation at Ex = 365 nm, yellow CDs and green N-doped CDs solutions exhibited weak and bright blue fluorescence, respectively. Photoluminescence excitation spectroscopy (PLE) and photoluminescence emission spectroscopy (PL) showed that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs and N-doped CDs were 395/460 nm and 345/445 nm, respectively. Photoluminescence blue shift caused by the doping of nitrogen is a normal phenomenon (Li et al., 2012). UV–vis spectra indicated that N-doped CDs solution had broader UV absorption in the range of 300–400 nm than CDs, and there were two distinct intrinsic peaks at about 235 nm and 333 nm. The broader and higher peaks in N-doped CDs can be attributed to the higher content of pyridinic N in the carbon core. The doping of nitrogen atoms in N-doped CDs was clearly shown in FTIR spectra and XPS survey (Fig. 1e and f). As shown in Fig. 1e, the difference of surface functional groups between CDs and N-doped CDs was mainly due to the doping of nitrogen atoms, which introduced new NCN groups (2140 cm−1) and enhanced N–H (3430 cm−1, 1630-1580 cm−1) and C–N groups (1200-1000 cm−1). In the XPS full spectrum of all samples (Fig. 1f), three main peaks corresponding to C 1s (284.1 eV), N 1s (400.6 eV) and O 1s (530.4 eV) can be observed. N 1s peak appearing in CDs can be attributed to air pollution. Compared with CDs (1.2), the C/O ratio in N-doped CDs was improved to 2.5, which can be attributed to the doping of 9.0% nitrogen atoms. The high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s was shown in Fig. S2. The N 1s XP spectra of N-doped CDs may be deconvoluted to two peaks at 399.6 and 401.2 eV, corresponding to the pyridinic N (C–N–H)/amide N(OC–NH) (53.2%) and pyrrolic N (C2–N–H)/graphitic N(N–C3) (46.8%). The N1s analysis demonstrated that different N species were involved in the N-doped CDs. The doping of N atoms promoted electron transition by providing additional electrons (Ali et al., 2022), which made N-doped CDs possess the excellent electrocatalytic ability (Fig. 1e–f and S3). The TEM image illustrated that the (N-doped) CDs were spherical and well dispersed (Figs. S4a–d). XRD and Raman spectroscopy confirmed that they had a similar structure to graphite, and N-doped CDs had higher graphitic degree (Fig. S4e-h). The particle size distributions of CD and N-doped CDs were 78–88% in the range of 0–15 nm with the average particle sizes of 4.57 and 6.41 nm, respectively (Fig. 1c and d). The results indicated that two type of carbon dots (<10 nm) with different surface properties and electrocatalytic properties were successfully synthesized.

With and without (N-doped) CDs addition, the OD600 and acetate accumulation of mixed culture acetogens on day 4 were 0.28–0.38 and 24.9–29.2 mM, respectively, indicating that CDs and N-doped CDs had good biocompatibility (Fig. 1g and h). As shown in Fig. 1i and j, with Ex = 395 nm, characteristic fluorescence emission peak of CDs at 460 nm was detected inside and outside the bacterial cells. With Ex = 345 nm, characteristic fluorescence emission peak of N-doped CDs at 445 nm was detected inside and outside the bacterial cells. As comparison, no fluorescence peaks were detected in CK with acetogens without modification, implying that CDs and N-doped CDs were successfully internalized by the bacterial cells. FTIR spectra showed that the peak intensity of functional groups on bacterial surface increased with increasing of incubation time, suggesting that the bacteria had good growth and metabolism characteristics after the modification (Figs. S5a and b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: (N-doped) CDs modified acetogens enhanced MES performance
ce:section.ce:para: After operation for nearly a month, the performance of MES within 4 cycles (16 d) is shown in Fig. 2 . Significant differences were observed in term of current output and acetate accumulation between MESs with CDs modified acetogens, N-doped CDs modified acetogens, and CK (p < 0.05, Table S1). With CDs and N-doped CDs modified acetogens, the maximum current of MES was 7.3 ± 0.8 and 7.1 ± 0.8 mA, which were 89–95% higher than that without modification (CK) (Fig. 2a), implying that modified living cell significantly enhanced the cathodic current. Besides, bare CDs and N-doped CDs in the catholyte did not increase the background current (Fig. S6), which is consistent with other studies. Those studies have confirmed that the background current caused by nanomaterials and dead cells with nanomaterials modified can be ignored (Liu et al., 2020). Therefore, the improved current could be attributed to modified living cell. Correspondingly, acetate accumulation on day 4 was 8.4 ± 0.6 and 7.7 ± 1.1 mM, which were 57–71% higher than that of CK (Fig. 2b). The average acetate production rate of the CK was 1.2 mM d−1, which is comparable with our previous studies (1.3 mM d−1 within 4 d), indicating the successful start-up of the MES in this study (Xiang et al., 2017). With CDs and N-doped CDs modified, the average acetate production rate of MES was 1.9–2.1 mM d−1, which was 58–75% higher than that of CK. The results indicated that the internalization of (N-doped) CDs by acetogens is an efficient strategy to enhance electron utilization in the MES.

As shown in Fig. 2c, the electron recovery efficiency of biocathode-MES increased from 73.3 ± 2.1% (CK) to 91.3 ± 2.1% (CDs) and 88.6 ± 4.0% (N-doped CDs) (p < 0.001), which were higher than that of cathode with conductive materials modification directly (51–70%) (Jourdin et al., 2014; Huang et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020). Conductive materials such as Mo2C and MoS2 were used for modifying cathode in the MES to catalyze H2 evolution, but their role might be limited by the utilization efficiency of H2 by the cells (Huang et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020). In this study, N-doped CDs modification slightly increased hydrogen evolution rate of cathode to 0.009 mM d−1, as compared with 0.007 mM d−1 in CK. The results indicated that conductive N-doped CDs may enhance the MES efficiency through the strategies other than hydrogen-mediated electron transfer, such as promoting electron mediator secretion (Liu et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020), interacting with cytochromes (Liu et al., 2015), activating EPS (Ye et al., 2018), etc.

The EIS fitting results of the cathodic biofilm are shown in Fig. 2d and e. With CDs and N-doped CDs modified, R p (ohmic impedance), R ct (interfacial transfer impedance) and R d (proton transfer impedance) decreased significantly. The total charge transfer impedance (R ct + R d) values of CDs and N-doped CDs biofilm were 138.1 and 51.6 Ω, respectively, which was 84–94% lower than that of CK (p < 0.001). The decrease percentage of charge transfer impedance was comparable to that by using conductive materials to directly modify the cathode (85.5%) (Song et al., 2020). The results showed that (N-doped) CDs integrated inside and outside bacterial cells reduced the charge transfer impedance between microbes and electrodes, and interspecies, enhanced the conductivity of cathodic biofilms, thereby improved the overall performance of MES.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofilm formation and EET mechanisms in the MES

ce:section.ce:para: After 50 d operation of the MES, CLSM was used to investigate the biofilm formation by acetogens internalized (N-doped) CDs. Cell coverage was calculated based on mean fluorescence intensity objecting from five random CLSM data of each sample (outliers were excluded). Fig. S7 showed that the CLSM measurement in this study had high repeatability, and the difference in biofilm formation between groups was statistically significant (p < 0.05). As shown in Fig. 3 a–c, thickness of cathodic biofilm increased from 22.1 ± 2.9 μm in CK to 41.0 ± 1.3 μm with CDs modified (p < 0.05). Correspondingly, the total biomass increased from 23.0 ± 0.4 mg L−1 in CK to 47.3 ± 1.3 mg L−1 with CDs modified (p < 0.001, Fig. 3d). With N-doped CDs modified, the thickness and biomass of biofilm were 29.7 ± 3.0 μm and 28.8 ± 0.6 mg L−1, respectively, which increased slightly compared with that in CK. Generally, the biofilm thickness of acetogenic biocathode was ∼28.43 μm in similar operation condition without any external factors (Xiang et al., 2017). For cathodic biofilm with CDs modified, the overall coverage was 112% times higher than that of CK (p < 0.0001), and the overall viability was consistent with CK (0.87). However, for cathodic biofilm with N-doped CDs modified, the overall coverage did not change, and the overall viability decreased to 0.52 (p < 0.05). The decline of viability is attributable to that nanomaterials can downregulate the expression of genes related to cell membrane and exoprotein synthesis (Shi et al., 2021). In this study, with CDs and N-doped CDs modified, the intracellular lactate dehydrogenase of cathodic biofilm decreased by 12.7% and 31.1%, respectively (Fig. 3d), suggesting the increase of cellular permeability. The enhancement of cellular permeability was not only beneficial to the uptake of nanomaterials in the surrounding medium, but also improved outward EET of the cells (Shen et al., 2014). Combining with the performance results (Fig. 2b and c), we reasonably believed that the internalized N-doped CDs might promote EET by changing the permeability of cells.

To further explore the effects of (N-doped) CDs on the long-distance electron transfer, the biofilms were divided into the inner layer, middle layer, and the outer layer, corresponding to 0–13 μm (close to the electrode), 13–29 μm (average division position), and 29–41 μm (close to the catholyte interface), respectively (Fig. 3e and f). Low coverage was observed in the inner layer, which was commonly observed in electroactive biofilms. Compared with CK, CDs increased the microbial viability in the inner layer by 23% (p < 0.05). The coverage rate of CDs in the middle layer was 63.2 ± 6.25%, which was 382% higher than that of CK (p < 0.05, Fig. 3e). Only the outer layer in CDs-modified biofilm had the coverage rate of 23.8 ± 1.4% and microbial viability of 0.78 ± 0.06 (Fig. 3f). These results indicated that CDs were beneficial to the biofilm construction, and promoted microbes far away from the surface of cathode to have high viability.

It has been shown that the presence of various active N-functionalities (pyrrolic, pyridinic, and graphitic N), not only facilitated CO2 adsorption and reduction in electrocatalytic process (Qin et al., 2020), but also promoted flavin -mediated and cytochrome-mediated outward EET process (Yu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020). To determine whether active N-functionalities of N-doped CDs were integrated into cathodic biofilm, the elemental composition and content of cathodic biofilm was analyzed (Fig. 4 ). The N content of cathodic biofilm with N-doped CDs modified increased by 38% (Fig. 4a). Pyridinic N (C–N–H)/amide N(OC–NH) (399.6 eV, 90.0%) dominated in the N 1s peaks of CK, while pyrrolic N (C2–N–H)/graphitic N(N–C3) (401.6 eV, 52.0%）occupied in the cathodic biofilm of N-doped CDs (Fig. 4b and c). The biofilm thickness in this study was 22.1–41.0 μm (Fig. 3a–c), while the XPS detection depth was only 3–5 nm. Therefore, the change of peak intensity can be attributed to cells attached to the surface of cathode. Based on the N 1s XP spectra of N-doped CDs (Fig. S2), the increasing content of pyrrolic N (C2–N–H)/graphitic N(N–C3) of cathodic biofilm may attribute to the integration of N-doped CDs into cells. Therefore, CDs enhanced the electrocatalytic performance of biofilms by increasing its biomass, while N-doped CDs improved the EET rate of biofilm by serving as “electron assistant”, which will be discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial communities in the cathodic biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of (N-doped) CDs on the composition of cathodic microbial communities was explored using three generations of full-length sequencing technology. In each sample, the effective reads accounted for ∼95% of the raw reads, and the coverage index remained at a high level (99.9%), indicating that the amount of sequencing data was sufficient and reliable (Table S3). Compared with CK, community richness (ACE) and community diversity (i.e., indexes of Simpson and Shannon) increased with CDs and decreased with N-doped CDs, indicating that CDs possessed higher α-diversity. Microbial community composition of cathodic biofilm at the genera level is shown in Fig. 5 a. Acetogens accounted for 55.0%, 45.0% and 67.1% of CK, CDs and N-doped CDs, respectively. Acetoanaerobium dominated in CDs and CK (22–26%), while Sporomusa was selectively enriched in N-doped CDs (42.5%). Microbial community composition and hierarchical cluster analysis at species levels further confirmed that the dominant species causing the difference in community compositions between CDs and N-doped CDs were Sporomusa paucivorans (14.6% vs. 39.7%), Acetoanaerobium sticklandii (22.1% vs. 15.3%), Acetobacterium wieringae (6.6% vs. 9.3%), and unclassified Proteiniclasticum (17.1% vs. 8.9%) (Fig. 5b and c). Sporomusa paucivorans have low DNA homology with extracellular respiratory bacteria—Sporomusa ovata, but have high DNA homology with extracellular respiratory bacteria—Sporomusa sphaeroides (Hermann et al., 1987). This implies that Sporomusa paucivorans may have the capbility of extracellular respiration. Acetoanaerobium sticklandii is a high ammonia-producing anaerobic bacterium capable of producing acetate using amino acids as the sole carbon and energy source (Stadtman and Mcclung, 1957). Acetobacterium wieringae has been found to exist on the surface of cathode and Fe (0) successively (Philips, 2019). The above-mentioned acetogens can utilize H2 to reduce CO2 for acetate production, but so far, the information about their presence in the cathodic biofilm is very limited. Proteiniclasticum can ferment amino acids to produce acetate (Zhang et al., 2010). Fermentative bacteria are common in acetogenic biocathode (Zhou et al., 2022), probably due to the fermentation of microbial soluble secretion. In addition, it is worth noting that Sporomusa sphaeroides has been confirmed to have the ability to reduce CO2 by extracellular uptake of electrons (Philips, 2019), and its relative abundance increased by 87–134% with N-doped CDs. In general, Acetobacterium and Acetoanaerobium belong to the Na + -dependent acetogens and lack cytochromes, while Sporomusa belongs to the H+-dependent acetogens, which contains cytochrome and H+-driven electron transport chain bound to the membrane (Rosenbaum and Müller, 2021). Gram-positive acetogens (Acetoanaerobium sticklandii, Acetobacterium wieringae) and gram-negative acetogens (Sporomusa sphaeroides, Sporomusa paucivorans) differ in their sensitivity to changes in solution conditions. Therefore, the difference in community composition between CDs and N-doped CDs is likely to cause their functional differences. KEGG analysis confirmed that N-doped CDs-mediated cathodic biofilms possessed higher functional abundance, including biosynthesis, signal transmission, carbon fixation, and carbon metabolism (Fig. S8). The results showed CDs did not change the composition of cathodic microbial community significantly. The differences in the composition and function of cathodic microbial community between CDs and N-doped CDs caused by Acetoanaerobium and Sporomusa, may be one of the important reasons affecting the performance of MESs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extracellular electron transfer strategies of the biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: PICRUSt2 was used to predict expression differences in functional genes participating in the electron transfer pathway (Table 1 ). With the CDs modified, the functional genes fccA and menG had 37–655% higher abundances than that with CK. Such genes are involved in the intermediate biosyntheses of soluble electron shuttles such as flavins and quinone, which could explain the enhanced thickness of cathodic biofilm with CDs modified. Compared with CK, the expression of CcdA encoding cytochrome c in Sporomusa was upregulated by 13.0% with N-doped CDs modified (Table 1). This suggested that the potential role of cytochrome c in inward EET may be stimulated by N-doped CDs. Additionally, pyrrolic N (C2–N–H)/graphitic N(N–C3) was introduced by N-doped CDs into cathodic biofilm and probably play an important role in electron transport (Fig. 4a–c). According to the literature, pyrrolic N was easily absorbed electrons kinetically from porphyrin-iron active centers of cytochromes c (Wang et al., 2020). Pyrrolic N accelerated the direct EET process of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 by promoting the interaction between OmcA/MtrC protein and anodic interface (You et al., 2016). In fact, most acetogens drive electron transport through Na+ or H+ pumps, and few acetogens possess cytochrome c (Rosenbaum and Müller, 2021). Therefore, the role of cytochromes c on the biocathode deserves further research. Furthermore, with N-doped CDs modified, the expression of cybH encoding cytochrome b, and cydB encoding cytochrome d in the Sporomusa was upregulated by 117% and 1616%, respectively. Membrane-bound cytochrome b was proposed as an electron carrier that transfers electrons to methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase in the membrane-bound energy storage respiratory chain for the reduction of CO2 to the methyl group of acetate (Kamlage et al., 1993). N-doped CDs might accelerate the electron transport rate of the cytochrome-dependent respiratory chain in acetogens to enable efficient acetate production by MES.

As shown in Fig. 6 a–c, two main peaks observed from the 3D-EEM fluorescence spectra of the extracted EPS from cathodic biofilm. Peak I (Ex/Em = 290/350–360 nm) and peak II (Ex/Em = 340–395/430-465 nm) were heme-like proteins and humic-like substances, respectively. Compared with CK, the normalized FI of peak I and peak II increased by 16–23% with CDs, while decreased by 17–60% with N-doped CDs (Table S2). Heme is iron protoporphyrin IX, which as essential active center of electron transfer of cytochrome c coordinates the reversible change between the reduced state (Fe2+) and oxidized state (Fe3+) (Hanna et al., 2017). Phenolic functional groups on quinones are the main electron donating and accepting moieties in humic acids, and it has been proved that quinones as an exogenous electron shuttle can accelerate extracellular electron transfer (EET) (Lovley et al., 1996). Herein, it is reasonably believed that the increasing secretion of heme-like proteins and humic-like substances in the EPS, can indirectly improve the electrocatalytic activity and electron transfer efficiency of cathodic biofilm with CDs modified.

As shown in Fig. 6d–f, the peaks with the maximum normalized FI were extracted from the 3D-EEM fluorescence spectra to reveal some covered components. In addition to heme-like proteins and humic-like substances, coenzyme F420 (Ex/Em = 420/470 nm) and flavins (Ex/Em = 420/510–520 nm) were also present in the extracted EPS from cathodic biofilm. The normalized FI of coenzyme F420 and flavins in the extracted EPS of cathodic biofilm increased by 18–25% with CDs, but decreased by 95–98% with N-doped CDs (Table S2). Internalized CDs can stimulate the secretion of flavin to promote the outward EET process of Shewanella strains (Yu et al., 2015). Pyrrolic N can anchor the diffused flavin on the bioanode, and the content of which has a good positive correlation with flavin/cytochrome c composites (Wang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020). The results suggested that internalized CDs may enhance the signal transduction between microbes by accelerating the secretion of flavin, thereby indirectly enhancing the formation of biofilm far away from the surface of cathode. Coenzyme F420 is present in hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Deng et al., 2021). Therefore, N-doped CDs may inhibit the ability of methanogens that compete with acetogens for electrons. Additionally, the introduction of nitrogen functionalities into cells by N-doped CDs may compensate for the lack of heme-like proteins and flavin, thereby improve the EET of cathodic biofilm.

In this study, the internalization of CDs and N-doped CDs by acetogens enhanced the inward EET and consequently the MES performance through a combination of multiple benefits. CDs enhanced the formation of biofilm by accelerating the secretion of redox active substances, such as flavins, heme protein and humic acid. CDs did not change the community structure, with Acetoanaerobium dominated the cathodic biofilm. N-doped CDs significantly shifted the community structure, with Sporomusa enriched in the biofilm, which maybe a result of the introduction of various active N-functionalities, which interacted with cytochrome and served as “electron assistant” to enhance the EET efficiency. Future work may involve comprehensive genomic analysis on pure strains of acetogens interacting with CDs and N-doped CDs, which will help elucidate the potential molecular biological mechanisms.

Jiaping Hu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, preparation. Cuiping Zeng: Methodology, Investigation. Guangli Liu: Writing – review & editing, and revising. Zhiyong Jason Ren: Writing – review & editing, and revising. Haiping Luo: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Min Teng: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 42077286, 42002303), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2023A1515011846).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced CO2 reduction and acetate synthesis in autotrophic biocathode by N-Hexanoyl-L-homoserine lactone (C6HSL)-based quorum-sensing regulation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, M.Z., Zeng, C.P., Liu, G.L., Luo, H.P., Zhang, R.D., 2022. Enhanced CO2 reduction and acetate synthesis in autotrophic biocathode by N-Hexanoyl-L-homoserine lactone (C6HSL)-based quorum-sensing regulation. Sci. Total Environ. 836, 155724-155733.
